claim: Claude.md serves four purposes: knowledge compression (summarizing workspace instead of reading every file), user preferences and conventions, declaration of capabilities (itemizing what agents can do), and a log of failures and successes to avoid repeated mistakes
actionable: Create a claude.md with sections for: workspace summary, your preferences (output format, coding style), declared capabilities (what Claude can do), and a running log of what doesn't work for future attempts.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Global Claude.md should contain high-level reasoning strategies and personal beliefs; local Claude.md should contain low-level workspace knowledge. Global is injected into every Claude Code session regardless of project; local is only loaded for specific projects.
actionable: Separate your Claude.md configuration into ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (global) and .claude/CLAUDE.md within each project folder (local). Global: your reasoning style, preferences, principles. Local: project-specific APIs, architecture, conventions.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: The /init slash command in Claude Code generates a claude.md by reading all files in your workspace and creating a highly compressed summary. This saves tokens on future queries by providing an index instead of requiring Claude to read entire files.
actionable: Run /init in any Claude Code workspace to auto-generate a claude.md. Review and customize it to match your preferences.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: When Claude lacks context about its own capabilities, it will refuse tasks or suggest building from scratch even when you have existing tools. Declaring capabilities explicitly in claude.md (e.g., 'you can call this API, you can work autonomously with browsers') prevents unnecessary clarification loops.
actionable: In your claude.md, explicitly list what capabilities Claude has access to: 'You have access to browser automation tools, can execute bash commands, can modify these APIs, can work autonomously for X minutes, etc.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: A local workflow for updating claude.md: plan feature → implement (encounter failures & successes) → compile learnings into claude.md → repeat. This loop accelerates subsequent feature development because Claude already has past learnings baked in.
actionable: After each feature development session, spend 5 minutes extracting what failed and what succeeded, then add a bullet-point summary to your claude.md under a 'Learnings' section. Before next feature, Claude will already know what to avoid.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: An agent harness is everything around an LLM that isn't the model itself: system prompts, tools, hooks, memory management, context limits, and parameter controls. It determines what an LLM can actually accomplish in the real world versus just communicate.
actionable: Think of Claude Code as a 'harness' around the Claude model. When optimizing Claude Code performance, focus not on the model (which you can't change) but on system prompts, tool selection, memory management, and context window usage.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Parallelization reduces total execution time significantly. A task with 4 sequential 5-minute steps (20 min total) can become 1 step (5 min) + 3 parallel steps (5 min) + synthesis (2 min) = 12 minutes, a 40% time reduction.
actionable: When Claude needs to do research, generate ideas, or review code, break it into parallel substeps. Use /agent teams or sub-agents to run 3-5 independent tasks simultaneously, then synthesize results in a final step.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Because LLMs are stochastic, running the same query 5 times produces 5 different answers that collectively explore more of the solution space. Stochastic consensus pools these answers to extract both high-agreement solutions and rare outliers.
actionable: When facing a complex design or optimization problem, use stochastic multi-agent consensus: spawn 5-10 agents with the same or slightly different framing to independently generate solutions, then aggregate by frequency. High votes = likely good; rare votes = creative outliers.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Debate model: spawn multiple agents in a shared conversation where they see each other's responses and refine their answers iteratively. This produces more nuanced solutions than independent stochastic consensus because agents incorporate peer feedback.
actionable: Use a debate skill to spawn 5-10 agents debating a problem in rounds. Each round, agents can see all prior responses and adjust. Run 3-5 rounds until consensus emerges. Results are more refined than single-run answers.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Fan-out/fan-in pattern: spawn multiple research sub-agents (often using cheaper Sonnet) to independently investigate different angles, then feed results to a synthesizer (Opus) to combine findings. This keeps context windows short (better quality) and costs less than serial research.
actionable: For research-heavy tasks, use fan-out/fan-in: prompt spawns 4-6 Sonnet agents to research different aspects in parallel, then a single Opus synthesizer integrates their findings into a final answer.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Model performance degrades as context window grows. Parallelization via sub-agents avoids this by keeping each agent's context short and in 'the zone of good' rather than pushing a single agent into poor-quality high-context territory.
actionable: When a task would result in a very long context window (>50k tokens), split it into parallel sub-agent tasks instead of running a single large task. Each sub-agent handles a smaller scope with shorter context.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Skills are self-contained SOPs in markdown files with metadata (name, description, prompt, tools). Sub-agents are essentially the same concept but with a fresher context window. Skills and sub-agents are increasingly converging as organizational primitives.
actionable: Use skills for reusable, frequently-called procedures (debate, research, analysis). Use sub-agents when you need a completely fresh context for a specific task. Both are markdown + tool definitions; they're interchangeable.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Parent-Researcher-QA pattern: a smart parent (Opus) orchestrates cheap researchers (Sonnet) for parallel investigation and a fresh QA agent for unbiased review. This balances capability with cost better than simple hierarchies like CEO-CTO-Engineer.
actionable: For complex projects, structure as: Opus parent -> delegates to Sonnet researchers -> QA agent (fresh Opus) reviews -> parent incorporates feedback in loop.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Auto-research requires three things: (1) a metric to optimize (e.g., Lighthouse score), (2) a change method (e.g., modifying code), (3) an assessment method (e.g., running a test). Faster change+assessment loops enable more iterations (up to 1,440/day if both take 30 sec).
actionable: To set up auto-research: define your metric (what are you improving?), the modification method (how Claude can change the code/system), and the assessment (how to measure improvement). Implement the loop in ~/auto-research/program.md.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Auto-research on Shopify's Liquid codebase achieved 53% faster parse+render time and 61% fewer object allocations through ~30 autonomous optimization iterations. Demonstrates auto-research can produce significant real-world improvements.
actionable: Reference Tobi Lutke's auto-research results as validation: if you set up correct metrics and change methods, expect 20-50% improvements on most optimization tasks.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: When modifying the same file many times, use a single write() call with the entire rewritten file rather than multiple sequential edit() calls. This is faster and uses far fewer tokens than line-by-line edits.
actionable: Add to your claude.md: 'When making widespread changes to a file, read it once, rewrite the whole thing in a single write() call instead of multiple edit() calls.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: When Claude fails at a task twice using Chrome DevTools MCP or browser automation, stop and ask the user before retrying. Repeated failures waste tokens; human input is more efficient than automated retry loops.
actionable: Add a rule to claude.md: 'If browser automation fails twice in a row, stop and ask the user rather than continuing to retry.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Never fetch well-known websites (like Apple.com) for reference when they're likely cached. Instead, reference them from memory or ask the user for specific design constraints. This saves tokens and speeds up execution.
actionable: Add to claude.md: 'Don't waste tokens fetching famous websites for design reference. Use existing knowledge or ask user for specific constraints.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Always read API documentation first before attempting to use a new platform. Spending tokens on docs reading prevents 99% of errors, saving far more tokens than the docs themselves consume.
actionable: Add to claude.md: 'When working with a non-trivial platform, always read API documentation first. If JS-heavy, use Chrome DevTools MCP. Reading docs saves 50x tokens versus debugging bad calls.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Claude consistently underestimates what it can do agentic-ally. If asked 'how long would X take to build?', Claude may say 3 months because it thinks in human terms, not realizing it can execute tasks autonomously in seconds/minutes.
actionable: When Claude estimates time for a task, adjust downward by 10-50x because it doesn't fully account for its own agentic capabilities. Be explicit: 'You can do this autonomously right now in seconds.'
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)

claim: Context window length scales inversely with output quality. Longer contexts = lower quality + higher cost. Compress knowledge (claude.md, summaries) and use parallelization to keep individual agent contexts under 30-50k tokens for best results.
actionable: Target keeping context windows under 30k tokens per agent. Use claude.md for compression, parallelize large tasks, and summarize intermediate results before passing to next stage.
source_title: CLAUDE CODE ADVANCED FULL COURSE (3 HOURS)
